The **1940 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), determined the resident population of the United States to be 132,164,569, an increase of 7.6 percent over the [1930 population](/wiki/1930_United_States_census "1930 United States census") of 122,775,046 people. The census date of record was April 1, 1940.

A number of new questions were asked including where people were five years before, highest educational grade achieved, and information about wages. This census introduced sampling techniques; one in 20 people were asked additional questions on the census form. Other innovations included a field test of the census in 1939. This was the first census in which every state (48) had a population greater than 100,000, and the first census to also include a "long form" (sent to only a subset of the households) with additional questions about socioeconomic and housing characteristics (the "long form" would be used for last time in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), being replaced by [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") afterwards).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Census questions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1940_United_States_census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Census questions")]

The 1940 census collected the following information:[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* address
* home owned or rented
  + if owned, value
  + if rented, monthly rent
* whether on a farm
* name
* relationship to head of household
* sex
* race
* age
* marital status
* school attendance
* educational attainment
* birthplace
* if foreign born, citizenship
* location of residence five years ago and whether on a farm
* employment status
* if at work, whether in private or non-emergency government work, or in public emergency work ([WPA](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration"), [CCC](/wiki/Civilian_Conservation_Corps "Civilian Conservation Corps"), [NYA](/wiki/National_Youth_Administration "National Youth Administration"), etc.)
  + if in private or non-emergency government work, hours worked in week
  + if seeking work or on public emergency work, duration of unemployment
* occupation, industry and class of worker
* weeks worked last year
* wage and salary income last year

In addition, a sample of individuals were asked additional questions covering age at first marriage, fertility, and other topics. Full documentation on the 1940 census, including census forms and a procedural history, is available from the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series").

## Data availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1940_United_States_census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Data availability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/1940_US_Census_Poster.jpg/250px-1940_US_Census_Poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:1940_US_Census_Poster.jpg)

1940 U.S. census poster

Following completion of the census, the original enumeration sheets were [microfilmed](/wiki/Microform "Microform"); after which the original sheets were destroyed.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

As required by [Title 13 of the U.S. Code](/wiki/Title_13_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 13 of the United States Code"), access to [personally identifiable information](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information") from census records was restricted for 72 years.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Non-personally identifiable information Microdata from the 1940 census is freely available through the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"). Also, aggregate data for small areas, together with electronic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System").

On April 2, 2012[[5]](#cite_note-5)—72 years after the census was taken—microfilmed images of the 1940 census enumeration sheets were released to the public by the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration").[[6]](#cite_note-weinstein1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-weinstein2-7) The records are indexed only by [enumeration district](/wiki/Enumeration_district "Enumeration district") upon initial release; several organizations are compiling indices, in some cases through [crowdsourcing](/wiki/Crowdsourcing "Crowdsourcing").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Use for Japanese American internment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1940_United_States_census&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Use for Japanese American internment")]

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the Census Bureau responded to numerous information requests from US government agencies, including the US Army and the US Secret Service, to facilitate the [internment of Japanese Americans](/wiki/Internment_of_Japanese_Americans "Internment of Japanese Americans"). In his report of the operation, U.S. Army Lt. Gen. [John L. DeWitt](/wiki/John_L._DeWitt "John L. DeWitt") wrote that "The most important single source of information prior to the evacuation was the 1940 Census of Population."[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
